text: is not involved in this, I can assure you. And I don’t want him to be any more than you do .” Her shoulders rose with a deep inhalation. A moment later, she released a slow, shuddering breath and opened her hand. The red spots on her fingers and palm faded away . “Come,” Ronin said . Chapter Seven N ever, ever trust them, Tabitha had told Lara after that horrifying night. If only they’d both taken the advice . Here Lara was again, doing exactly what she knew not to . Ronin waited behind her, and she found his patience infuriating. It would be so much easier to hate him if he’d just push her. If he’d show his true colors and reveal the monster under his fake skin . Could she trust him to keep his word? His exchange with the gearheads had been different from what she’d seen between other bots. He seemed…evasive, almost confrontational. And he claimed Warlord wasn’t involved. Why be honest about meeting with the ruler of Cheyenne only to lie about the nature of their chat ? Ronin’s behavior wasn’t typical of the bots she’d dealt with. It was too human, and that was unsettling Finally, she turned and tipped her head back, looking up into his face. He watched her with that unblinking stare. The corners of his lips were downturned; was that worry in his expression ? “Well?” she asked. A tiny crease formed between his brows. “We going? I’m tired of being wet .” Ronin’s gaze lingered on her. After a while, he turned and walked away from the gate . Lara followed, head bowed with eyes on her feet to keep the rain out of her face. She was soaked to the bone, her sopping clothes chafing her skin. Her boots were waterlogged, squishing with each step. And, to top it all off, the temperature was dropping rapidly with the coming sunset . She opened her pack and stuffed the food inside. Then she wrapped her arms over her chest, tucked her hands into her armpits, and clung to whatever warmth she could muster . When she glanced up to make sure Ronin was still ahead of her, something at the edge of her vision caught her attention. She turned her head, breath fleeing her lungs. Lara had never seen so much greenery. Beyond the road, the ground was blanketed in lush grass, which in turn was bordered by tall, living trees. Their leaves swayed in the wind, shimmering as they turned from one side to the other . She walked to the edge of the field and crouched. Extending her hand, she ran her palm over the grass. It tickled her skin. She pressed down, testing its give, amazed at how soft it was . “I think this is how much of the world used to look,” Ronin said from the street . “Why does it only look like this here ?” “Because bots…maintain. Many of us exist only to repair what is broken. To make it appear whole again .” She plucked a few blades of grass and raised them to her nose, inhaling. “It smells so fresh.” Her hands were red and approaching numbness, but what did that matter, in the face of such wonder ? “Come. We need to get you out of the rain .” Despite everything, Lara was reluctant to move on. She ran her gaze over the seemingly endless green. What would it look like during the day, with the sun shining? If she walked away, would it still be here when she returned ? “You can see it from my residence .” “But not smell it.” She stood up and faced Ronin . “When it’s trimmed, you can .” She wasn’t entirely sure what he meant, but she didn’t bother asking. Warmth. That had to be her focus . They continued along the street, the green landscape remaining to their left as the path curved to the right. Up ahead were more lights, brighter and somehow cleaner than the orangey-yellow ones here. It was a familiar glow — she saw it every night from her little shack on the other side of the wall . Ronin led her around another bend in the road, and suddenly it opened into a wide, long stretch lined with buildings on both sides. Not the pieced-together, rickety little shacks the humans lived in; these were real buildings, made of bricks and wood and concrete, with their glass windows intact and their doors actually attached to their hinges . This was nothing like what she’d seen. Even the buildings in the Market couldn’t compare to the pristine majesty of the Bot District . Had it truly been this way everywhere, before everything fell apart? Even the ruins she normally scavenged? It seemed impossible, but the proof was here, right before her eyes . Still, something was wrong. Everything was too perfect, too clean, too quiet. Things existed here, but nothing lived . The buildings reminded her of the bots. Well-maintained and varied, no two quite the same, but all similar nonetheless . She glanced at Ronin. Maybe not all bots … He lifted an arm and pointed down a street branching to the left. Compared to the main road, it was dark and narrow. The street lamps, spaced further apart, only ran down the right side of the road. On the opposite side, trees stood in silent vigil as far as she could see. Strange that it still seemed inviting. More natural . “Not much farther,” Ronin said . Lara was so lost, gazing at the greenery across the street, that she almost bumped into Ronin when he stopped. She looked around, shaking off her confusion. Up ahead, the street they were on ended, meeting with a road running east to west. Beyond that loomed another portion of the wall . “This way.” Ronin stepped through a gate in a high wall of bushes .
